meta.about: Edit projects and reviews here. After changes, run node scripts/sync-site-data.js. That refreshes assets/js/site-data.js so the site works when opening index.html from disk (file://) and stays in sync on GitHub Pages.

card.subtitle: Health programmes · event lists in Excel
card.title: One-Click Scraper for American Heart Association
card.summary: Pulled public workshops and events from the association’s site into a clean spreadsheet with dates, places, and organisers so their team could plan outreach instead of retyping pages. One click to run; hours saved each week.

detail.subtitle: Less copy-paste for the American Heart Association
detail.title: One-Click Scraper for American Heart Association
overview: The team needed an accurate list of public workshops and events from their site: dates, places, and who was running each session, so they could invite the right people and plan outreach without retyping everything.

Before we spoke, someone was doing it manually: open a page, copy a block, fix mistakes, repeat. I built a simple one-click tool that gathers those listings overnight (metaphorically speaking, often minutes) and drops them into a tidy spreadsheet their non-technical staff already knew how to use.
They stopped losing half a day to grunt work. What they got was a repeatable file they could filter, print, or hand to marketing with the same columns every time and no mystery gaps.

timeline.label: What “good” looked like
timeline.text: We lined up the fields they cared about and checked a short sample together so everyone agreed on the format before the full pull.

timeline.label: Quiet background work
timeline.text: The tool ran through event pages the way a careful intern would, just faster, and parked the results in Excel so nothing overwrote last week’s export by mistake.

timeline.label: Real-world messiness
timeline.text: Odd venues, duplicate headings, slow pages: we decided in advance what to skip vs. flag so the sheet stayed trustworthy.

timeline.label: They could run it alone
timeline.text: Short notes on how to open it and rerun after the website changed shape; their tech contact got a link if they ever wanted to peek under the hood.

card.subtitle: Game studio · art & UI in one Drive folder
card.title: Game App Assets Extraction
card.summary: Gathered sprites and UI graphics from a mobile title into a single, well-labelled share on Google Drive so designers could reuse assets without digging through builds.

detail.subtitle: Art and UI files, ready for the design team
detail.title: Game App Assets Extraction
overview: The client had a mobile game but the art lived in bits and pieces across builds, hard to hand to designers or reuse in trailers.
I pulled the images and related metadata into one clean folder on Google Drive, with labels so you could find “this button” or “that background” without installing the game five times.
They went from screenshots and guesswork to a single delivery they could forward to a studio and say “everything’s in here.”

timeline.label: What to keep vs. toss
timeline.text: We agreed on naming and which file types mattered so nothing important got merged into fluff.

timeline.label: One organized pass
timeline.text: A single run collected the batch without duplicating huge folders if we had to rerun after an update.

timeline.label: Spot-checks against the live app
timeline.text: Random samples so blurry or cropped assets got caught before handoff, not in Slack at midnight.

timeline.label: Drive link + short walkthrough
timeline.text: They got the folder structure, a brief inventory, and a demo clip so non-technical folks weren’t lost.

card.subtitle: Online retail · full catalog export
card.title: Complete eCommerce Data Extraction
card.summary: Built a complete product catalog (titles, variants, images, attributes) in one structured file on Drive so pricing and merchandising could move faster than copy-paste from the storefront.

detail.subtitle: Store catalog in one place, not fifty tabs
detail.title: Complete eCommerce Data Extraction
overview: They were running an online shop workflow where prices, pictures, and variants changed faster than anyone could paste into Excel.

I delivered a full product catalog export with titles, attributes, and image links so they could compare runs over time, feed another tool, or onboard a new VA without training them on the storefront’s back office.
The win was a complete dataset on Drive instead of a half-filled sheet the day before a launch.

timeline.label: Must-have columns
timeline.text: We separated what the business couldn’t live without from nice-to-haves so the first delivery was already usable.

timeline.label: One category first
timeline.text: Proved volume and accuracy on a slice before scaling to the whole catalog. Fewer surprises for everyone.

timeline.label: Safer pacing
timeline.text: Handled big pulls in chunks with checkpoints so a hiccup didn’t mean starting from zero.

timeline.label: Files + honest notes
timeline.text: Packaged the export with a short list of quirks (e.g. old SKUs) so imports didn’t blindside them.

card.subtitle: Member directory · from app to spreadsheet
card.title: Data Extraction from Onebound Android App
card.summary: Exported the app’s community profiles, name, role, company, short bio, into Excel so the client could analyse and reach out without scrolling hundreds of cards by hand.

detail.subtitle: Member profiles off the phone and into a spreadsheet
detail.title: Data Extraction from Onebound Android App
overview: They had a community directory inside an Android app, names, roles, companies, short bios, and leadership wanted it in rows for reporting and partner outreach.
There was no friendly “download CSV” button. I delivered a structured Excel file that matched how they already work: filter by company, sort by role, mail-merge if they want.
The payoff was hours of scrolling turned into something their ops team could actually analyse.

timeline.label: Stable starting point
timeline.text: Locked the app version and flow so later runs didn’t suddenly point at the wrong screen.

timeline.label: Walking the member list
timeline.text: Captured each profile methodically, including longer “about” text, without smushing the columns.

timeline.label: Sample sign-off
timeline.text: They checked a slice, tweaked labels once, then we ran the full set.

timeline.label: Workbook + context
timeline.text: Final Excel plus a one-pager on how to refresh later if the directory grows.

card.subtitle: Shift workers · fewer missed check-ins
card.title: Auto Attendance Login/Logout
card.summary: Put clock-in and clock-out on a reliable rhythm tied to policy, less scrambling at shift change and fewer “oops” conversations with HR.

detail.subtitle: Fewer missed punches for shift workers
detail.title: Auto Attendance Login/Logout
overview: Check-in rules were tied to showing up in the right place at the right window. Human error meant HR back-and-forth and sometimes unfair flags.
I automated the sequence so the punch happened in policy, without someone standing by the phone at 8:59. The team still owned the rules; the boring part got consistent.
They spent less time fixing mistakes and more time on actual operations.

timeline.label: When automation is allowed
timeline.text: Wrote down the time bands and scenarios so we didn’t step outside what HR was comfortable with.

timeline.label: Test account first
timeline.text: Proved a clean check-in/out on a sandbox before touching real schedules.

timeline.label: Real life: slow networks, restarts
timeline.text: Built in retries and sane logging: enough to debug, not a novel.

timeline.label: Show, don’t mystify
timeline.text: Short demo clip + a single-page runbook for whoever inherits it.

card.subtitle: Retail promos · codes from sheet to app
card.title: Automated Coupon Handling Bot For Mobile App
card.summary: Tied a shared spreadsheet to the mobile checkout flow so promo codes applied in bulk with clear success/fail rows, less typing, fewer wrong redemptions.

detail.subtitle: Promo codes applied faster, fewer typos
detail.title: Automated Coupon Handling Bot For Mobile App
overview: Discount codes lived in a spreadsheet, but someone still had to read each one, type it in the app, and confirm, slow and easy to mess up at volume.
I connected that sheet to the app workflow: fresh codes get picked up, applied in order, and the sheet updates with what worked or failed. Alerts drew eyes when something broke mid-run.
Merchandising kept control from the spreadsheet; engineering didn’t need a new dashboard for every campaign.

timeline.label: Statuses everyone understands
timeline.text: Simple columns: pending, used, invalid, sortable without a training session.

timeline.label: Read vs. apply
timeline.text: Split the work so a hiccup in one step didn’t trash the other.

timeline.label: Night runs without drama
timeline.text: Capped aggressive retries so the app didn’t get hostile while still finishing overnight batches.

timeline.label: What to do when it stalls
timeline.text: Alert path back to the sheet plus a short “first checks” note.

card.subtitle: Professional directory · contacts in one file
card.title: National Council Mobile App Data Extraction
card.summary: Turned a mobile-only membership directory into a filterable contact list with names, titles, firms, emails, and phones so staff could work from a spreadsheet instead of endless in-app search.

detail.subtitle: Contact directory without manual lookup marathons
detail.title: National Council Mobile App Data Extraction
overview: They needed names, titles, firms, emails, and phone numbers that only appeared inside a mobile directory. Great for members, painful for ops without an export.
I produced a consolidated workbook so they could filter by region or role and stop click-copying hundreds of cards.
Think of it as turning “find each person in the app” into “open this file and search.”

timeline.label: Where each field hides
timeline.text: Not every contact showed email the same way. We mapped taps vs. plain display before scaling.

timeline.label: Tidy phone and title lines
timeline.text: Light cleanup so Excel sorting didn’t look like alphabet soup.

timeline.label: Full pass with resumes
timeline.text: Long runs could pause; we didn’t restart from scratch when the app napped.

timeline.label: One row per person + oddities tab
timeline.text: Partial rows landed in a side sheet so nothing silently disappeared.

card.subtitle: Fantasy sports · live stats for their own models
card.title: Dream11 Basketball Data API
card.summary: Supplied fresh player stats through match night so their dashboards and prediction sheets updated without someone refreshing public pages by hand.

detail.subtitle: Live basketball numbers for their own tools
detail.title: Dream11 Basketball Data API
overview: A fantasy-style client wanted player stats updating through match night for their internal sheets and models, not by refreshing public pages by hand.
I gave them a small feed they could query like any other data service: reliable shape, sane errors when the source hiccuped, no babysitting tabs.
Their analysts spent energy on judgement calls, not on whether the last refresh was five minutes stale.

timeline.label: What “fresh enough” means
timeline.text: We picked refresh expectations before the first slate so nobody argued at halftime.

timeline.label: Steady updates without hammering
timeline.text: Paced pulls so numbers felt live without picking fights with rate limits.

timeline.label: Predictable responses
timeline.text: Simple read endpoints plus logging hooks for “why is this blank?” support.

timeline.label: If the feed goes quiet
timeline.text: Where to look first, how to restart, so they weren’t blocked on my timezone.

card.subtitle: Game content · prompts ready for editors
card.title: Truth & Dare App Data Extraction
card.summary: Pulled every truth question, dare, and level bump out of the shipped game into spreadsheets, easier for localization, marketing, and legal review than playing through levels with a notepad.

detail.subtitle: Game text out of the build and into edit-friendly sheets
detail.title: Truth & Dare App Data Extraction
overview: Questions, levels, and dares lived only inside the shipped game, localization and marketing needed them in neutral spreadsheets for review.
I exported the content into workbooks editors could comment on row by row instead of playing through dozens of levels with a notepad.
Version-to-version diffs became practical when the publisher tweaked copy.

timeline.label: Every place copy lived
timeline.text: Mapped screens by level bands so nothing hid in a corner case menu.

timeline.label: Structured passes
timeline.text: Separated truth prompts vs. dare prompts instead of dumping everything in one blurry column.

timeline.label: Duplicates and odd characters
timeline.text: Flagged repeats and encoding glitches before localization vendors saw them.

timeline.label: Tabs per content type + README
timeline.text: Delivered files their PM could forward without a follow-up call.

card.subtitle: Ethical-investing list · watch without daily drudgery
card.title: Zoya Stock App Automation
card.summary: Monitored a curated stock roster in the Zoya app and surfaced meaningful changes by email, so the client didn’t open the same screens every morning just to see if anything moved.

detail.subtitle: Compliance-friendly list tracking without daily eye strain
detail.title: Zoya Stock App Automation
overview: The client cared about a curated, rules-based stock list inside a niche app. Opening it every morning to see what moved wasn’t sustainable.
I mirrored how they filtered and read the list, then diffed runs over time and pinged them when something meaningful changed, not every tiny UI quirk.
Net: less manual reopening, earlier notice when the roster shifted.

timeline.label: Their actual morning routine
timeline.text: Recorded the taps and filters they trusted so automation matched human judgement.

timeline.label: Scheduled scans
timeline.text: Compared today vs. yesterday so “what’s new?” was obvious.

timeline.label: Alert tuning
timeline.text: Cut noise so cosmetic text changes didn’t cry wolf.

timeline.label: After an app redesign
timeline.text: Left notes on refreshing the flow when buttons moved, so they weren’t stuck.
